The facts of this case are, I think, a very good example. That is definitely a preferred plan than anything else, again, because those creditors who failed to file proofs of claim, they're gone. Tens of thousands of dollars of debt.

No. But this case is different because it has a beginning, middle, and an end, and it's a discrete proceeding. Now, is that, in your opinion, the correct test for sifting among contested matters? Are there other contested matters, where appeal, as if they were final orders, is allowed? What is the criterion? Will it, in fact, produce a nightmare? You're going to say, yes, but I mean, I'd like to know why. MR. HALLWARDбґDRIEMEIER: Well, Your Honor, I guess I would start by answering that I don't think that contested matter is the бґбґ the standard.

It can be many, many бґбґ

turns.text: Am I right about бґбґ on the standard of what counts as a final order for appeal? I can't бґбґ I don't want to put words in your mouth бґбґ MR. HALLWARDбґDRIEMEIER: We бґбґ

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: бґбґ but that I can say there is basic agreement there. MR. HALLWARDбґDRIEMEIER: Well, I бґбґ I would say that this бґбґ there бґбґ there has been in the briefing, at least, this amount of disagreement between us. We think that the word "finality" бґбґ or "final," rather, as used in Section 158, the bankruptcy appeal provision, is the same as Б─°finalБ─² as it's used in бґбґ in 1291 for civil appeals, that the judicial unit is different. It can be a proceeding as opposed to the whole case. But Б─°finalБ─² has been interpreted to mean terminates the judicial unit. The бґбґ the Court has, in a sense, dissociated бґбґ it's not, we're бґбґ we're done with this one now. Okay? They have suggested that Б─°finalБ─² may mean something a little bit broader in bankruptcy or that the use of the word "order" instead of "decision" in bankruptcy may be something different. That argument there which бґбґ and they've invoked this notion of flexible finality бґбґ that should send off alarm bells in the Court's mind, because flexible finality is basically the same as a practical construction of finality, which is the clause that spawned the Collateral Order Doctrine which the Court has spent the last 30 years trying to put back in a box. And that is really the problem here; that unless there is a clear rule бґбґ and we think it's the statutory language proceeding. We think Section 157 provides a list of such proceedings, and for these purposes, it's бґбґ

turns.text: Well, but that бґбґ MR. HALLWARDбґDRIEMEIER: бґбґ confirmation of a plan.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But that is your бґбґ it's still begging the question, because they're saying the бґбґ the proceeding is the confirmation of a plan, and if the confirmation of the plan is a denial, that бґбґ that ends that proceeding. So you are begging the question. MR. HALLWARDбґDRIEMEIER: Well, I бґбґ I Б─⌠ I don't think so, Your Honor, because I think that бґбґ I'm drawing the analogy to 1291. If you have a plan that's denied confirmation with leave to amend, that's not final. If you have a claim, a proof of claim, that is rejected with leave to amend or a claim of priority that isn't rejected with leave to amend, that's not final in our view. On their view, I don't know, because they assume that if you had a proof of claim, and it was denied with leave to amend, that must be immediately appealable as final, because now the creditors' preferred view of their claim has been rejected. Or maybe their rule is бґбґ I don't know бґбґ only if the basis of the denial was a rule of law. But their бґбґ I think they rightly understand that you can't have a rule of finality that turns on whether it is or is not a rule of law, so as this Court's precedent instructs, we decide as a category. And as a category, plans are denied multiple times within a bankruptcy, many times for minute, factбґbased reasons. And, again, the incentive structure that would be created бґбґ we've talked so far about Chapter 13 pretty exclusively, but the бґбґ the Petitioner agreed that the rule would likely apply to Chapter 11, as well. In Chapter 11, things are quite different. In Chapter 11, the debtor is often the debtorбґinбґpossession is the estate. They're playing with house money. They're spending the creditors' money because the administrative expenses of the estate read the lawyers' fees come off of the estate. So for them to take an appeal on any issue that they can, you know, reasonably claim a legitimate dispute about and thereby prolong their control over the бґбґ of the estate, prolong the date when distributions will be made бґбґ

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: And they have resources to do it. MR. HALLWARDбґDRIEMEIER: And they have the resources to do it.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: And Chapter 13 doesn't? MR. HALLWARDбґDRIEMEIER: That's right. But the rule is going to be the same. And in Chapter 13, what we've seen бґбґ we didn't see the бґбґ the бґбґ the big numbers. We did see over seven hundred reported, or бґбґ or at least on Westlaw, decisions in appeals that had been pursued pro se. So an individual debtor who is intent on preserving their ability to stay in the house certainly could take a number of appeals. Again, this case, I think, proves the point. This case started with an objection by the Trustee to the fact that this Debtor claimed an automobile ownership cost, the same one that was ultimately rejected by the Court in Ransom. That issue could have been taken up on immediate appeal. So it's бґбґ it's a significant concern, I think, and, again, because you're making these determinations on the basis of the category, it's important to remember that even those factual disputes could be бґбґ and when we think of, you know бґбґ which do we think is бґбґ is бґбґ is more unlikely? That the debtors are going to exercise restraint. Trust the debtors. They won't take appeals when they shouldn't be taken. Or do we trust the courts to apply the rules, the very liberal rules, for interlocutory review. And to do that бґбґ

turns.text: That no бґбґ

The courts have their own interest. The court of appeals have an interest in not being forced to resolve issues before they need to be resolved because some of those issues are going to go away. If there is an appeal from a later plan that's ultimately confirmed, I would suspect strongly that there will be many disputes along the way that will have fallen out either because they became moot бґбґ

turns.text: I thought that was the reason that бґбґ that the бґбґ the бґбґ all are debating about what the incentives are. And бґбґ and they say the bankruptcy judges are all over the lot on some of this, and they'd like to generate some appeals because they believe that then you would have a more uniform law. It would be easier for the creditors as well as the debtors. And I think that's their basic argument in their brief. MR. HALLWARDбґDRIEMEIER: It бґбґ it бґбґ it is their argument and бґбґ

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: I бґбґ I don't know where to put that бґбґ MR. HALLWARDбґDRIEMEIER: Well бґбґ

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: бґбґ in this бґбґ MR. HALLWARDбґDRIEMEIER: I guess I look at this Court's docket and it seems to me that bankruptcy cases are fairly well represented in the appellate courts. I бґбґ I do agree that there are issues on which parties would like to get reviewed, but Congress has considered that very issue. And in the statute бґбґ in 2005, at a time when it was effectively 5бґ0 our way on this issue. The only question was the Fifth Circuit. But, again, they had suggested that if leave was given to amend, it would not be final. When Congress looked at it in that context, they didn't change the final order rule which had been construed by five courts of appeals at that time. What they did was they created a new avenue for interlocutory appeal direct to the court of appeals precisely to get that kind of guidance, but бґбґ but they gave the power to the court of appeals to decide whether the issue was coming up бґбґ

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: So one бґбґ one thing. If you were бґбґ maybe there's an argument against this бґбґ but suppose for argument's sake I agreed with you on the basic point. It would then seem important to put in the opinion there is a problem here about there being insufficient appeals to generate law, but there is a mechanism, namely, the interlocutory mechanism, which perhaps has been used too sparingly. Now, what do you think of that? MR. HALLWARDбґDRIEMEIER: I бґбґ I think that would be very wise on the Court's part.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: That was Justice Kagan's point initially. MR. HALLWARDбґDRIEMEIER: Because бґбґ and бґбґ and I think we should бґбґ should recall that under Section 158, the first level of review which is exercised by the district court over interlocutory orders is really plenary. They can review any interlocutory order of the bankruptcy court.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You know, sometimes they even ignore our holdings. Do you think they're not going to ignore this бґбґ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: бґбґ this бґбґ this piece of advice? MR. HALLWARDбґDRIEMEIER: Well, Your бґбґ Your Honor бґбґ MR. HALLWARDбґDRIEMEIER: I think that the бґбґ I think that this бґбґ this approach is the same one the Court took in Mohawk. In Mohawk, the Court said, No, we're not going to create an appeal as of right to challenge in order to disclose one's attorneyбґclient confidential material, but бґбґ but the courts should apply the rules of interlocutory appeal or mandamus in a way бґбґ

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: And did they take our advice? MR. HALLWARDбґDRIEMEIER: Well, I have not бґбґ I have not researched that, Your Honor. But to conclude, I think that the critical question is what's the proceeding. And the proceeding as set forth in 157(b)(2) is confirmation of plans. That is the core purpose of a Chapter 13 or a Chapter 11. Until that happens, there are no legal rights that are affected. That's the difference between denial of plan confirmation with leave to amend and confirmation. One sets rights. Of course, it's immediately appealable. Res judicata, even, under this Court's decision in Espinosa, but denial of plan confirmation with leave the amend maybe even, as Your Honor suggested earlier бґбґ Justice Breyer, suggesting precisely how to amend in order to get it confirmed, that is not immediately appealable. And I don't think that the Court should rely on the selfбґrestraint of the parties to avoid the problems with that problem. Instead, it's proper use of the tools that Congress gave the courts in the 2005 amendments. If there are no further questions, thank you.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Feldman, two minutes.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Thank you. For бґбґ just one thing on бґбґ on Section 158(a), the plenary, the бґбґ so what my friend said was plenary right of interlocutory review, actually every court that has looked at that has said бґбґ they applied the same standard as under 1292(b). But the main point here is that what the debtor is faced with is two very, very difficult roads to actually getting review of what could be an important legal decision. On the dismissal side, in addition to the problems caused by losing the automatic stay and trying to convince the court to grant a stay, there's also the disability of if you file бґбґ file within бґбґ what is it? A year, I think. The automatic бґбґ if you refile a case, you only get an automatic stay for 30 days. And there's other disabilities that we talk about in our brief. So that is a very hard thing to impose on somebody where the creditor can make бґбґ take an appeal just as of right because they don't like what the confirmed plan said. On the other hand, as far as trying to confirm a later plan, what this forces the debtor into is a situation where a debtor may have to try to figure out and propose a plan that the debtor might even prefer not to be in bankruptcy at all rather than to live under that plan. But yet, the only way for the debtor to get the review of the earlier denial of confirmation is to propose some plan that maybe disposes of property in a way that the debtor completely doesn't want and would rather have just given up on the whole thing. Nonetheless, they have to propose the plan, wait for objections, litigate it through, get it confirmed, and then appeal that. And that бґбґ those are severe burdens to put on the debtor. Now, every бґбґ the reasons why Bank of America and the United States are here, I think, is that there is a need for bankruptcy precedent to be developed. And one problem is that debtors in this situation are not able to get the review of the court of appeals, which is their only way of getting actually to an Article III judge at all. And they're not able to get that, and for those reasons, we ask that the decision of the court of appeals should be reversed.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
